notes:
Chapter processed in two reading passes merged into one sequence (dense chapter, 68 paragraphs, ~8.8k words). The chapter is unusually quote-dense (Miller's autobiography plus extensive embedded Scripture citations); following the granularity precedent set in gc_ch17.claims.json, embedded Bible quotations attributed to named biblical figures/documents are extracted as attributed_quote (or event where they report a narrated action) with needs_verification true, since misquotation/unlocatable-attribution is a defined verdict category. Sweeping doctrinal/typological framing and supernatural narration are logged as theological with needs_verification false. The central Millerite chronological chain (457 BC decree -> AD 27 baptism -> AD 31 crucifixion -> AD 34 end of the seventy weeks -> 1844) is broken into discrete date/causal claims since this is the load-bearing historical argument of the chapter and of the book's central thesis.

searches: ~5, clusters: 6 (Miller biography via local Bliss; Millerite 457BC->1844 chronology chain [doctrinally central]; 1833 Leonid meteor storm [doctrinally central]; Josiah Litch/Ottoman 1840 [doctrinally central]; postmillennialism history; medieval vernacular Bible access via local Deanesly [doctrinally central]). Bible-text attributed_quotes resolved via KJV common-knowledge fast-path (empty sources). Biography cluster leans on local primary Bliss (Memoirs of William Miller 1853) which White drew from. Control sample of 3 needs_verification:false claims: GC-18-049 (no smuggled fact -> unverifiable), GC-18-091 (smuggles verifiable biblical+biographical facts -> supported), GC-18-122 (smuggles biographical fact -> supported). Consistency: 457 vs 458 BC split treated as disputed per ch22 verifier.

claims.quote: Like many other reformers, William Miller had in early life battled with poverty, and had thus learned the great lessons of energy and self-denial.

verification.rationale: Bliss records Miller's father as Captain William Miller, an officer of the Revolutionary period (later also a militia/regular-army captain). Standard in the insider biography.

claims.quote: “Annihilation was a cold and chilling thought, and accountability was sure destruction to all. The heavens were as brass over my head, and the earth as iron under my feet. Eternity—what was it? And death—why was it? The more I reasoned, the further I was from demonstration. The more I thought, the more scattered were my conclusions. I tried to stop thinking, but my thoughts would not be controlled. I was truly wretched, but did not understand the cause. I murmured and complained, but knew not of whom. I knew that there was a wrong, but knew not how or where to find the right. I mourned, but without hope.”
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted describing his state of despair and confusion as a religious skeptic before his conversion.

verification.rationale: Passage from Miller's own conversion narrative as preserved in Bliss's Memoirs; accurately attributed.

sources.title: S. Bliss, Memoirs of William Miller (1853)

claims.quote: “Suddenly,” he says, “the character of a Saviour was vividly impressed upon my mind. It seemed that there might be a being so good and compassionate as to himself atone for our transgressions, and thereby save us from suffering the penalty of sin. I immediately felt how lovely such a being must be, and imagined that I could cast myself into the arms of, and trust in the mercy of, such a one. But the question arose, How can it be proved that such a being does exist? Aside from the Bible, I found that I could get no evidence of the existence of such a Saviour, or even of a future state....
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted describing a sudden impression of the character of a Saviour, and his realization that no evidence for such a being existed apart from the Bible.

verification.rationale: Miller's own words on the sudden impression of a Saviour, from his apology/autobiography as preserved in Bliss.

sources.title: S. Bliss, Memoirs of William Miller (1853)

claims.quote: “I saw that the Bible did bring to view just such a Saviour as I needed; and I was perplexed to find how an uninspired book should develop principles so perfectly adapted to the wants of a fallen world. I was constrained to admit that the Scriptures must be a revelation from God. They became my delight; and in Jesus I found a friend. The Saviour became to me the chiefest among ten thousand; and the Scriptures, which before were dark and contradictory, now became the lamp to my feet and light to my path. My mind became settled and satisfied. I found the Lord God to be a Rock in the midst of the ocean of life. The Bible now became my chief study, and I can truly say, I searched it with great delight. I found the half was never told me. I wondered why I had not seen its beauty and glory before, and marveled that I could have ever rejected it. I found everything revealed that my heart could desire, and a remedy for every disease of the soul. I lost all taste for other reading, and applied my heart to get wisdom from God.”(517)
claims.claim_text: William Miller is quoted describing his conclusion that the Bible must be a divine revelation and his newfound delight in studying Scripture.

verification.rationale: Miller's own words on concluding the Bible was a divine revelation; preserved in Bliss.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Miller's statement on the fulfilled chronology of Scripture is from his apology as preserved in Bliss; the quotation attribution is accurate (the underlying prophetic-year interpretation is assessed separately in this cluster).

claims.quote: “Seventy weeks are determined upon thy people and upon thy holy city.... Know therefore and understand, that from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem unto the Messiah the Prince shall be seven weeks, and threescore and two weeks: the street shall be built again, and the wall, even in troublous times. And after threescore and two weeks shall Messiah be cut off, but not for Himself.... And He shall confirm the covenant with many for one week: and in the midst of the week He shall cause the sacrifice and the oblation to cease.”
claims.claim_text: The angel is quoted (Daniel 9:24-27) as telling Daniel that seventy weeks are determined upon the Jewish people and Jerusalem, that from the commandment to restore and build Jerusalem to Messiah the Prince would be sixty-nine weeks, that Messiah would be cut off after those weeks, and that in the midst of the final week the sacrifice and oblation would cease.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Daniel 9:24-27, correctly attributed.

claims.quote: The word here translated “determined,” literally signifies “cut off.”
claims.claim_text: The Hebrew word translated 'determined' in Daniel 9:24 literally signifies 'cut off.'
claims.extractor_note: This is a checkable linguistic/translation claim about the Hebrew term in Daniel 9:24, not a sweeping value judgment; flagged for verification despite the 'interpretive' tag, since no better-fitting enum value exists in the schema.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Hebrew nechtak/chathak (Dan 9:24) is a hapax legomenon whose root sense is 'to cut, cut off/divide'; standard lexicons (Gesenius, BDB) give this literal derivation. The contextual gloss 'determine/decree' is compatible with a root meaning 'cut off'.

claims.quote: Seventy weeks, representing 490 years, are declared by the angel to be cut off, as specially pertaining to the Jews.
claims.claim_text: According to the text's interpretation of Daniel 9, the seventy weeks (490 years) are declared to be 'cut off' as a period specially pertaining to the Jewish people.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The seventy weeks = 490 years (year-day) 'pertaining to thy people' (Dan 9:24) is the traditional messianic-prophetic reading, standard in evangelical scholarship (e.g. Hoehner). claims.quote: “Allowing the first period, 150 years, to have been exactly fulfilled before Deacozes ascended the throne by permission of the Turks, and that the 391 years, fifteen days, commenced at the close of the first period, it will end on the 11th of August, 1840, when the Ottoman power in Constantinople may be expected to be broken. And this, I believe, will be found to be the case.”(560)
claims.claim_text: Josiah Litch is quoted calculating, based on periods of 150 years and 391 years and fifteen days, that Ottoman power in Constantinople would be broken on August 11, 1840.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Litch's calculation (150 years + 391 years 15 days) and the quotation are genuinely his, but the precise 'August 11, 1840' pin-point is a contested Millerite proof: critics note Litch's 1838 prediction gave the looser 'August 1840', that the day-precise figure was refined afterward, and that Litch himself later abandoned the fulfillment claim. Genuine historiographical dispute over the exactness.
verification.modern_consensus: Litch's Aug-11-1840 precision and its 'exact fulfillment' are disputed; Litch dropped the claim in later life.

sources.title: Josiah Litch - Wikipedia

claims.quote: At the very time specified, Turkey, through her ambassadors, accepted the protection of the allied powers of Europe, and thus placed herself under the control of Christian nations.
claims.claim_text: At the time specified by Josiah Litch's calculation (August 1840), Turkey, through her ambassadors, accepted the protection of the allied powers of Europe.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. In the 1840 Oriental Crisis the Ottomans did accept the intervention of the allied European powers (Convention of London, signed July 15, 1840; ultimatum to Muhammad Ali delivered in August), so a real event underlies the claim. But characterizing this as the predicted 'overthrow' of Ottoman power is contested: the Empire did not fall (it endured until 1922), the Convention preceded Aug 11, and the 'at the very time specified' fulfillment is a Millerite interpretation, not a historical consensus.
verification.modern_consensus: The 1840 events were a Great-Power intervention preserving, not overthrowing, the Ottoman Empire; the 'exact fulfillment' framing is disputed.

claims.quote: While the papal church withholds the Bible(569) from the people, Protestant churches claimed that an important part of the sacred word—and that the part which brings to view truths specially applicable to our time—could not be understood.
claims.claim_text: The Roman Catholic Church withholds the Bible from the laity, and, comparably, Protestant churches of the Millerite era claimed that the prophetic portions of Scripture could not be understood.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-full. Deanesly's standard monograph documents late-medieval ecclesiastical prohibitions of vernacular Bible reading and 'the withholding of the scriptures from' the laity (esp. after the Waldensian/Lollard movements, e.g. the 1408 Oxford constitutions). The historical core is well supported. Caveat: Deanesly nuances the blanket generalization: there was no single, timeless, universal ban on the Bible itself; restrictions targeted unauthorized vernacular translations and varied by region and period. The present-tense sweeping polemic overstates that documented reality.
verification.modern_consensus: The medieval church restricted unauthorized vernacular Bibles in specific times/regions rather than imposing a universal ban on Scripture.
